content.title: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Syncope
content.description: A report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines and the Heart Rhythm Society.
content.collaboration: Developed in collaboration with the American College of Emergency Physicians and Society for Academic Emergency Medicine.
content.endorsement: Endorsed by the Pediatric and Congenital Electrophysiology Society.

publication.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

content.committee: Writing committee members include several doctors, chaired by Win-Kuang Shen, MD, FACC, FAHA, FHRS.

content.Title: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline

Subsections.Title: Initial Evaluation of Patients With Syncope

Task Force Members: Glenn N. Levine, MD, FACC, FAHA, Chair
Patrick T. O’Gara, MD, MACC, FAHA, Chair-Elect
Jonathan L. Halperin, MD, FACC, FAHA, Immediate Past Chair
Sana M. Al-Khatib, MD, MHS, FACC, FAHA
Kim K. Birtcher, MS, PharmD, AACC
Biykem Bozkurt, MD, PhD, FACC, FAHA
Ralph G. Brindis, MD, MPH, MACC
Lee A. Fleisher, MD, FACC, FAHA
Susan J. Pressler, PhD, RN, FAHA

content.Preamble: Since 1980, the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) have translated scientific evidence into clinical practice guidelines to improve cardiovascular health. These guidelines are based on systematic methods to evaluate and classify evidence, providing a cornerstone for quality cardiovascular care. They are official policy of the ACC and AHA.
content.Intended Use: Guidelines apply to patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, focusing on medical practice in the United States, but also have a global impact. They are intended to improve patients’ quality of care, aligning with patient interests and should not replace clinical judgment.
content.Clinical Implementation: Effective management occurs when recommendations are followed by healthcare providers and patients, emphasizing shared decision making.
content.Methodology and Modernization: The ACC/AHA Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines continuously updates and modifies guideline methodology, incorporating evolving technologies and increasing dissemination efficiency.

content.Overview: The document discusses guidelines designed to ensure recommendations remain current, emphasizing cost-value considerations and the review of new data on an ongoing basis. Updates occur approximately every six years, with new study results necessitating evaluation by the Task Force.
content.Selection of Writing Committee Members: The Task Force emphasizes diverse backgrounds, integrating various geographic regions, sexes, ethnicities, and intellectual perspectives.
content.Relationships with Industry: Strict policies are in place to avoid bias from industry and other entities, with full disclosure of relationships and relevant interests.
content.Evidence Review: Recommendations are developed using evidence-based methodologies, focusing on randomized controlled trials and other types of studies. An independent evidence review committee (ERC) is convened when necessary.
content.Guideline-Directed Management and Therap: Includes clinical evaluation and procedural treatments, stressing the need for reviewing dosage and treatment regimens.
content.Class of Recommendation and Level of Evi: Defines the strength of recommendations, based on the quality of evidence and estimated magnitude of benefit.

content.Purpose: The guidelines provide recommendations based on clinical evidence for strategies, interventions, treatments, or diagnostic testing in patient care.

Class of Recommendation (COR).Class IIa (Moderate): Is reasonable and can be useful.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class IIb (Weak): May be considered but with uncertain usefulness.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class III: Harm (Strong): Potentially harmful and should not be performed.

Level of Evidence (LOE).Level A: High-quality from multiple randomized controlled trials.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-LD: Limited data from observational or registry studies.

content.Methodology: Recommendations are evidence-based, derived from a comprehensive literature review of human subject research.

content.Title: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline
content.Purpose: To provide contemporary, accessible, and succinct guidance on the management of adult and pediatric patients with suspected syncope.
content.Reviewed By: The document was reviewed by various relevant organizations, including the ACC, AHA, HRS, and others.
content.Scope: Intended for cardiologists, arrhythmia specialists, neurologists, emergency physicians, general internists, geriatric specialists, sports medicine specialists, and other healthcare professionals involved in syncope care.
Goals: Define syncope as a symptom with different causes.
Provide guidance on evaluation and management of patients with suspected syncope.
Identify key areas lacking knowledge to foster research opportunities.
Considerations: Includes risk stratification and prevention of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in certain populations.
Focuses on patient-centered management rather than large segments of health services.
Key Points: The guideline involves a systematic review of clinical questions.
It includes the management of syncope in special populations like older adults and those with underlying cardiac conditions.
The writing committee was composed of clinicians with expertise in syncope.

sections.title: ACC/AHA guideline policy relevant to the management of syncope

guidelines.title: Device-based therapies for cardiac rhythm abnormalities
Ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death
Scientific statement on electrocardiographic early repolarization
Expert consensus statement on the diagnosis and treatment of postural tachycardia syndrome, inappropriate sinus tachycardia, and vasovagal syncope

definition.title: Definitions: Terms and Classification
definition.content: The guideline defines relevant syncope terms in Table 3.

epidemiology_and_demographics.title: Epidemiology and Demographics
epidemiology_and_demographics.content: Syncope has various causes, with reported prevalence rates as high as 41%, and a recurrent syncope occurrence between 13-56%. In a study of 1,925 randomly selected residents of Olmsted County, Minnesota with a median age of 62 years, 364 reported episodes of syncope in their lifetime. Female prevalence was higher than male (22% vs. 15%). There is a trimodal age incidence with peaks at early adulthood, middle age, and older age.

terms.Syncope: A symptom that presents with an abrupt, transient, complete loss of consciousness, associated with inability to maintain postural tone, with rapid and spontaneous recovery. The presumed mechanism is cerebral hypoperfusion.
terms.Loss of consciousness: A cognitive state in which one lacks awareness of oneself and one's situation, with an inability to respond to stimuli.
terms.Transient loss of consciousness: Self-limited loss of consciousness that can be divided into syncope and nonsyncope conditions. Nonsyncope conditions include hypoglycemia, metabolic conditions, or intoxication.
terms.Presyncope (near-syncope): The symptoms before syncope, which may include lightheadedness, visual sensations like 'tunnel vision.'
terms.Unexplained syncope: Syncope for which a cause is undetermined after an initial evaluation.
terms.Orthostatic intolerance: Symptoms that occur with standing and are relieved by sitting or lying down.
terms.Orthostatic tachycardia: An increase in heart rate of >=30 bpm within 10 mins of standing.
terms.Orthostatic hypotension: A drop in blood pressure >=20 mm Hg systolic or >=10 mm Hg diastolic within 3 min of standing.
terms.Neurogenic OH: Due to dysfunction of the autonomic nervous system.
terms.Cardiac syncope: Caused by bradycardia, tachycardia, or low cardiac index.
terms.Reflex syncope: Due to a reflex that causes vasodilatation, bradycardia, or both.
terms.Vasovagal syncope: The most common form of reflex syncope, occurring due to emotions, pain, or medical settings.
terms.Carotid sinus syndrome: Reflex syncope associated with carotid sinus hypersensitivity.
terms.Situational syncope: Reflex syncope associated with specific actions like coughing or swallowing.
terms.Postural tachycardia syndrome (POTS): A clinical syndrome with standing-induced symptoms like palpitations or fatigue.
terms.Psychogenic pseudosyncope: A syndrome of apparent but not true loss of consciousness.

content.syncope_incidence_increase: Sharp increase in incidence after 70 years of age.
content.types_of_syncope: Reflex syncope is the most common (21%), followed by cardiac syncope (9%) and orthostatic hypotension (OH) (9%).
content.HF_association: Syncope is present in 12% to 14% of patients with New York Heart Association class III-IV HF.
content.hospitalization_data: 7% annual incidence of syncope in older institutionalized adults, 23% overall prevalence, and a 30% 2-year recurrence rate.
content.prevalence_of_syncope: Ranges from 0.8% to 2.4% in studies; higher prevalence in older adults.
content.ED_data: 6.7 million syncope episodes in emergency department (ED), 0.77% of all ED patients.
content.falla_Predisposition: Older adults are prone to falls, with a 1-year fall rate of 38% among fainters versus 18.3% among nonfainters.
content.initial_evaluation: Focus on history, physical examination, and ECG (Class I).
content.evaluation_flowchart: Starts from transient loss of consciousness, leading to potential treatment or further evaluation.

content.recommendation: A detailed history and physical examination should be performed in patients with syncope.
important_factors: Prognosis, diagnosis, reversible or ameliorable factors, comorbidities, medication use, and patient/family needs.
Cardiac syncope carries a significantly worse prognosis than neurally mediated syncope.
Family history with emphasis on histories of syncope or sudden unexplained death.
diagnostic_focus: Bystander observations, vital signs, and post-event symptoms.
physical_examination: Orthostatic blood pressure and heart rate changes.
Presence of murmurs, gallops, or rubs suggestive of structural heart disease.
historical_characteristics: Older age (≥60 y) is associated with cardiac causes of syncope.
Family history of inheritable conditions or premature sudden cardiac death.

ECG Evaluation.Initial Assessment: In patients with syncope, a resting 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) is advised to identify potential causes such as arrhythmias. It offers a way to detect underlying arrhythmogenic substrates.
ECG Evaluation.Interpretation: While ECG is helpful in identifying possible causes of syncope, its prognostic value remains debated. An abnormal ECG with specific conditions can indicate an increased risk of death within one year.

Risk Assessment.Purpose: Evaluate short- and long-term morbidity and mortality risks associated with syncope, considering the cause and underlying conditions.
Risk Assessment.Application: Risk stratification should be used carefully, taking into account patient-specific factors and comorbidities. Clinical judgment is necessary for effective evaluation.

History: Outpatient Clinic or ED Evaluat: Family history of SCD (sudden cardiac death)
Absence of nausea/vomiting preceding syncopal event
VA (ventricular arrhythmia/ventricular arrhythmias noted in table)

Table 5 Short- and Long-Term Risk Factor.Notes: Table provides short- and long-term clinical risk factors from outpatient/ED evaluation and physical/lab findings associated with adverse outcomes in syncope.

Table 6 Examples of Syncope Risk Scores.Predictors: Abnormal ECG; >45 y of age; VA; HF
Abnormal ECG; >65 y of age; HF
Abnormal ECG; >65 y of age; no prodrome; cardiac history
Abnormal ECG; dyspnea; hematocrit; systolic BP <90 mm Hg; HF

Symptoms of acute coronary syndrome; worrisome cardiac history; family history of SCD; VHD; signs of conduction disease; volume depletion; persistent abnormal vital signs; primary central nervous event
Abnormal ECG/cardiac history; palpitations; exertional; supine; precipitant (a low-risk factor); autonomic prodrome (low-risk factors)
Abnormal ECG; trauma; no prodrome; male sex
Abnormal ECG; >90 y of age; male sex; positive troponin; history of arrhythmia; systolic BP >160 mm Hg; near-syncope (a low-risk factor)
Abnormal ECG; B‑type natriuretic peptide; hemoglobin; O2Sat; fecal occult blood

Table 7.Purpose: Examples of Serious Medical Conditions That Might Warrant Consideration of Further Evaluation and Therapy in a Hospital Setting.

Cardiac Arrhythmic Conditions: Symptomatic conduction system disease or Mobitz II or third-degree heart block
Symptomatic bradycardia or sinus pauses not related to neutrally mediated syncope
Inheritable cardiovascular conditions predisposing to arrhythmias
Noncardiac Conditions: Major traumatic injury due to syncope

Additional Evaluation and Diagnosis.Overview: Selection of diagnostic tests after initial history, physical examination, and baseline ECG is based on patient's clinical presentation, risk stratification, and diagnostic/prognostic value of testing.
Additional Evaluation and Diagnosis.Recommendations: Broad-based use of additional testing is costly and often ineffective; recommendations are provided for appropriate syncope evaluation.
Additional Evaluation and Diagnosis.Figure 3: Algorithm for additional evaluation and diagnosis for syncope.

genes.context: HCM indicates hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; it's listed under cardiac or vascular nonarrhythmic conditions that may require further evaluation.

Blood Testing Recommendations.Biomarkers: Availability of simple and accurate biomarkers might streamline risk stratification and diagnosis of syncope. Circulating biomarkers are evaluated as markers of hypotension or underlying disease processes, but none have met with strong success.

Targeted Blood Tests.Description: Targeted blood tests are reasonable in evaluating selected patients with syncope based on clinical assessment. Broad-panel testing is common, but data is lacking on its utility. Specific testing should be based on history and physical examination when syncope presentation or associated comorbidities suggest testing is necessary.

Brain Natriuretic Peptide and High-Sensi.Description: Usefulness in patients with suspected cardiac syncope is uncertain. Elevated natriuretic peptide is observed in patients with syncope determined to be cardiac. Yet, contemporary troponin measurement finds only limited value unless myocardial infarction is suspected.

Routine Lab Testing.Description: Routine and comprehensive lab testing is not useful in patients with syncope. Specific cardiac biomarkers may have a limited diagnostic role, but broad-panel testing lacks linkages to syncope presentation or mechanism.

Cardiovascular Testing Recommendations.Overview: Cardiovascular causes of syncope are common, often associated with poor prognosis. Testing can be crucial in evaluating and managing selected patients, emphasizing clinical judgment in selecting cardiovascular testing pathways.

content.Transthoracic echocardiography: This can be useful in selected patients with syncope if structural heart disease is suspected. It is noninvasive and low risk, useful for detecting conditions like aortic stenosis, HCM, or LV dysfunction.
content.CT/MRI: May be useful in selected patients with syncope of suspected cardiac etiology. Reserved for cases where noninvasive means are inconclusive. Useful for delineating cardiovascular anatomy.
content.Routine cardiac imaging: Not useful for evaluating syncope unless cardiac etiology is suspected based on history, examination, or ECG.

3.2.2. Stress Testing Recommendations.Exercise stress testing can be helpful t: 3.2.3. Cardiac Monitoring Recommendations: The choice of cardiac monitor type should depend on the patient's frequency and nature of syncope episodes. Several ambulatory monitoring techniques are available, such as Holter monitors and patch recorders. Technology advances offer more strategic monitoring, specifically external loop recorders for identified arrhythmic etiology.

genes.context: Exertion can result in syncope in a variety of conditions...such as LQTS (type 1) and catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT).

content.clinical_guideline: To evaluate selected ambulatory patients with syncope of suspected arrhythmic etiology, an implantable cardiac monitor (ICM) can be useful. Several RCTs and observational studies have demonstrated a benefit of the ICM in establishing a diagnosis in syncope of unclear etiology. A study showed a 19% diagnostic yield with conventional testing versus ICM, indicating higher diagnosis rates with the use of ICM.
content.cost_effectiveness: The cost per diagnosis was lower in patients who received ICM than in patients who underwent conventional approaches. Differences in healthcare settings, patient populations, and technology changes are key considerations in cost assessment.

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: A portable, battery-operated device with continuous recording for 24-72 hours, up to 2 weeks with newer models.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Symptoms frequent enough to be detected within a short period (24-72 h of monitoring).

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: Transmits patient-activated data to a remote location.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Frequent symptoms, likely to recur within 2-6 weeks. Limited use in patients with sudden incapsulated syncope.

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: Records and stores rhythm data for weeks to months.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Frequent, spontaneous symptoms related to syncope.

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: Continuously records and stores rhythm data, with patient-trigger capability.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Can be considered as an alternative to an external loop recorder.

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: Records and transmits data up to 30 days from preprogrammed arrhythmias.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Spontaneous symptoms related to syncope and rhythm correlation.

cardiac_rhythm_monitors.description: Subcutaneously implanted device with a battery life of 2-3 years.
cardiac_rhythm_monitors.patient_selection: Recurrent, unexplained syncope after a nondiagnostic initial workup.

In-Hospital Telemetry.Recommendation: Continuous ECG monitoring is useful for hospitalized patients admitted for syncope evaluation with suspected cardiac etiology.
Rationale: Patients with syncope and structural heart disease are at high risk of death or significant arrhythmia.
Inpatient telemetry can be a valuable diagnostic modality.
The diagnostic yield is low without suspicion of an arrhythmic cause.
Study Results: Diagnostic yield in 18% of patients with 15% demonstrating bradyarrhythmias.
8% prevalence of arrhythmias in patients without heart disease.
Telemetry may not be cost-effective for patients without suspected cardiac etiology.

Electrophysiological Study.Role: Limited in evaluating syncope, especially in patients without heart disease.
Benefits: Identifies certain bradyarrhythmias or tachyarrhythmias as syncope cause.
Inducible ventricular tachycardia in patients with syncope is predictive of prognosis.
Limitations: The role of EPS diminished due to the use of ICD for preventing SCD.
ICDs may reduce risk of death but not prevent syncope.

content.EPS recommendation: EPS is not recommended for syncope evaluation in patients with a normal ECG and normal cardiac structure and function, unless an arrhythmic etiology is suspected.
content.Diagnostic yield: Proactive evaluation of 247 patients showed higher diagnostic yield with an abnormal ECG (22%) than with a normal ECG (2.6%).
content.Tilt-table testing utility: Tilt-table testing can be useful for patients with syncope and suspected VVS when diagnosis is unclear after initial evaluation.
content.Positive response definition: Defined as inducible presyncope or syncope with hypotension, with or without bradycardia.
content.Optimal tilt table angle: A tilt-table angle of 70 degrees for 30 to 40 minutes is optimal.
content.Delayed OH: Tilt-table testing useful for patients with syncope and delayed OH when initial evaluation is not diagnostic.

Recommendation for Autonomic Evaluation.Recommendation: Referral for autonomic evaluation can be useful to improve diagnostic and prognostic accuracy in selected patients with syncope and known or suspected neurodegenerative disease.

content.Details on Neurogenic OH: The care of patients with neurogenic OH is complex. Selection of treatments needs to be balanced against the increased morbidity of not treating symptoms. Neurologists, cardiologists, internists, or other knowledgeable physicians should provide care. Syncope due to neurogenic OH is caused by central or peripheral autonomic nervous system damage or dysfunction, including conditions like Parkinson’s disease, central nervous system disorders, peripheral autonomic dysfunction, or neuropathies.

Recommendations for Neurological Diagnos.Recommendation: Simultaneous monitoring of an EEG and hemodynamic parameters during tilt-table testing can be useful to distinguish among syncope, pseudosyncope, and epilepsy.

MRI and CT Use.Recommendation: MRI and CT of the head are not recommended in the routine evaluation of patients with syncope in the absence of focal neurological findings or head injury that support further evaluation.

content.Neurological and Imaging Diagnostics: Routine neurological testing is of very limited value in syncope evaluation unless specific conditions are met.

content.Carotid artery imaging: Not recommended for routine evaluation of patients with syncope without focal neurological findings indicating further evaluation.
content.EEG recording: Not recommended for routine evaluation of patients with syncope without specific neurological features suggestive of a seizure.
content.Management of cardiovascular conditions: Recommendations align with ACC/AHA guidelines, specifically in the context of syncope.
content.Identification of arrhythmic etiology: Important for patients with syncope, especially in cases involving cardiac arrhythmias.
content.Evaluation of syncope associated with br: GDMT recommended. RCT shows dual-chamber pacemaker reduces recurrent syncope.
content.Adenosine triphosphate: Evidence evolving in syncope evaluation; no US consensus on use.

Notes: Patients with SVT often manifest palpitations and lightheadedness, while syncope is uncommon.
Older patients with paroxysmal SVT are more prone to syncope than younger patients.
Evaluation should differentiate arrhythmic syncope from nonarrhythmic syncope.
Patients with VA may present with syncope that is nonsustained or sustained.
Syncope from VA is multifactorial and depends on severity of the underlying cardiac disease.

Syncope in patients with underlying hear: ACC/AHA guidelines were reviewed to ensure current practices remain effective.

Ischemic and Nonischemic Cardiomyopathy.Recommendation: In patients with syncope associated with ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy, GDMT is recommended.
Ischemic and Nonischemic Cardiomyopathy.Additional Information: Evaluation involves diagnosis and prognosis. Treatment is based on the specific cause of syncope and associated cardiomyopathy. ICD is recommended for syncope of undetermined origin in patients with VA, and for unexplained syncope with nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy.

Valvular Heart Disease.Recommendation: In patients with syncope associated with valvular heart disease, GDMT is recommended.
Valvular Heart Disease.Additional Information: Patients with aortic stenosis may experience syncope due to hemodynamic causes. Aortic valve replacement is recommended for severe aortic stenosis and syncope.

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).Recommendation: In patients with syncope associated with HCM, GDMT is recommended.
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).Additional Information: Unexplained syncope is a predictor for SCD and ICD discharges. ICD implantation is recommended for HCM with recent syncope episodes of arrhythmic nature.

Recommendations.Recommendation: ICD implantation is recommended in patients with ARVC who present with syncope and have a documented sustained VA.
ICD implantation is reasonable in patients with ARVC who present with syncope of suspected arrhythmic etiology.
ICD implantation is recommended in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis presenting with syncope and documented spontaneous sustained VA.
In patients with cardiac sarcoidosis presenting with syncope and conduction abnormalities, GDMT is recommended.
ICD implantation is reasonable in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis and syncope of suspected arrhythmic origin, particularly with LV dysfunction or pacing indication.
EPS is reasonable in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis and syncope of suspected arrhythmic etiology.

Inherited Arrhythmic Conditions.Studies: Few, mostly open label or non-randomized
Inherited Arrhythmic Conditions.Associated Risks: Increased risk of SCD, cardiac arrest, overall cardiac death
Inherited Arrhythmic Conditions.ICD: Effective in reducing cardiac arrest but impact on syncope unknown

Brugada Syndrome.Definition: Genetic disease with increased risk of SCD
Brugada Syndrome.Characteristic: ST elevation in right precordial leads V1 and V2

ICD Implantation.Not Recommended: Patients with reflex-mediated syncope or absence of risk factors

EPS.May Be Considered: To assess syncope mechanism due to arrhythmia

Short-QT Syndrome.Description: Short-QT syndrome is a genetic disease characterized by palpitations, syncope, and increased risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD), associated with QTc interval <340 ms.
Short-QT Syndrome.Prognosis: Limited data are available about its prognostic significance, particularly without documented ventricular tachycardia (VT) or ventricular fibrillation (VF). The prevalence is very low, ranging from 0.02% to 1.63%.
Short-QT Syndrome.Recommendations: ICD implantation may be considered in patients with short-QT pattern and syncope of suspected arrhythmic etiology. Quinidine therapy might offer some protection, but data are insufficient to make any recommendations.

Long-QT Syndrome.Diagnosis: LQTS is diagnosed with a QTc >500 ms or LQTS risk score >=3.5 when secondary causes are excluded or in presence of a pathogenic mutation in one of the LQTS genes.

Management.Syncope: Recognition and treatment are needed to avoid recurrences, which could present as cardiac arrest or SCD. Avoidance of strenuous activities and drugs that prolong QT interval is recommended.
Management.Beta-Blockers: Indicated as first-line therapy in patients with LQTS and suspected arrhythmic syncope. Efficacy depends on genotype.
Management.ICD Implantation: Recommended for those who do not respond to beta-blockers or cannot tolerate them.

genes.context: LQTS is diagnosed in the presence of a pathogenic mutation in 1 of the LQTS genes.

content.Clinical Guidelines: The document outlines recommendations for managing patients with Long QT Syndrome (LQTS) and Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT). The primary interventions include Left Cardiac Sympathetic Denervation (LCSD) for patients intolerant to beta-blocker therapy and specific recommendations for CPVT management focusing on exercise restriction, beta-blocker therapy, flecainide, and ICD therapy.

genes.context: Mutation in the gene encoding the cardiac ryanodine receptor (RyR2) (autosomal dominant) or in the cardiac calsequestrin gene (CASQ2) (autosomal recessive) is linked to CPVT.
Mutation in the gene encoding the cardiac ryanodine receptor (RyR2) (autosomal dominant) or in the cardiac calsequestrin gene (CASQ2) (autosomal recessive) is linked to CPVT.

recommendations: In patients with CPVT who continue to experience syncope or VA, verapamil with or without beta-blocker therapy may be considered.
Verapamil helps suppress arrhythmias and delay the onset of exercise-induced ventricular ectopy.
LCSD may be reasonable in patients with CPVT, syncope, and symptomatic VA despite optimal medical therapy.
CPVT.context: Patients with CPVT may receive verapamil to suppress arrhythmias and LCSD if syncope occurs despite optimal medical therapy.

early_repolarization_pattern.description: Characterized by a J point and ST elevation, prevalent in young athletes and can increase risk of cardiac death.
recommendations: ICD implantation may be considered in patients with early repolarization pattern and suspected arrhythmic syncope in the presence of a family history of early repolarization pattern with cardiac arrest.
ICD implantation may also be considered if there's a family history of unexplained SCD, VF, or polymorphic VT with documented early repolarization pattern in the family.
EPS should not be performed in patients with early repolarization pattern and history of syncope without other indications.

content.title: 5. Reflex Conditions: Recommendations
content.summary: Vasovagal syncope (VVS) is the most common cause of syncope and a frequent reason for ED visits. It results from reflex causing hypotension and bradycardia. An episode is associated with prodrome of diaphoresis, warmth, pallor, and fatigue. Management includes patient education, physical counter-pressure maneuvers, and medications like midodrine and fludrocortisone.

recommendations.recommendation: Patient education on the diagnosis and prognosis of VVS.
Physical counter-pressure maneuvers for patients with VVS with a sufficiently long prodromal period.
Midodrine for patients with recurrent VVS without hypertension, HF, or urinary retention.
Uncertain usefulness of orthostatic training for patients with frequent VVS.
Fludrocortisone might help patients with recurrent VVS and inadequate response to salt and fluid intake.

content.5.2. Pacemakers in Vasovagal Syncope: Re: Pacemakers may be beneficial for patients with bradycardia and asystole during vasovagal syncope spells. It's crucial to select patients based on documented asystole. A positive tilt-table test may identify patients benefiting from pacing. Recommendations are based on a systematic review.
content.5.3. Carotid Sinus Syndrome: Recommendat: Carotid sinus syndrome involves mechanical manipulation of the carotid sinus, leading to syncope. It's diagnosed through reproduction of syncope during carotid massage. More common in men over 40 due to baroreceptor or possibly medullary dysfunction. Pacemaker therapy is considered for patients with cardioneurogenic or mixed forms of the syndrome.

content.Other Reflex Conditions: Situational syncope is defined as syncope occurring only in certain situations, like micturition, defecation, cough, and more. Investigations should determine any underlying causes. Treatments often focus on avoiding or eliminating triggering events. Recommendations include reducing salt consumption and removing or reducing hypotensive drugs and diuretics.

Neurogenic Orthostatic Hypotension Recom.Recommendation: Acute water ingestion is recommended for temporary syncope relief in neurogenic OH.
Counter-pressure maneuvers can benefit patients with neurogenic OH.
Compression garments can benefit patients with syncope and OH.
Midodrine can benefit patients with syncope due to neurogenic OH.

recommendations.statement: Droxidopa can be beneficial in patients with syncope due to neurogenic OH.
recommendations.details: Droxidopa improves symptoms of neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (OH) due to Parkinson's disease, pure autonomic failure, and multiple system atrophy. Side effects include headache, dizziness, and nausea.

recommendations.statement: Fludrocortisone can be beneficial in patients with syncope due to neurogenic OH.
recommendations.details: Fludrocortisone increases plasma volume, improving symptoms of OH. It can cause edema, hypokalemia, and headache. Serious adverse reactions may occur with doses >0.3 mg daily.

recommendations.statement: Encouraging increased salt and fluid intake may be reasonable in selected patients with neurogenic OH.
recommendations.details: Salt and fluid supplementation may improve blood pressure and decrease symptoms of OH. High salt intake increases blood pressure via a pressor effect.

recommendations.statement: Pyridostigmine may be beneficial in patients with syncope due to neurogenic OH who are refractory to other treatments.
recommendations.details: Pyridostigmine improves orthostatic tolerance by increasing peripheral vascular resistance and blood pressure. Side effects include nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, sweating, salivation, and urinary incontinence.

recommendations.statement: Octreotide may be beneficial in patients with syncope and refractory recurrent postprandial or neurogenic OH.
recommendations.details: Octreotide reduces splanchnic blood flow, increases blood pressure, and improves orthostatic tolerance. It prevents postprandial hypotension.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses syncope related to medication, highlighting its prevalence in older adults with comorbidities. It emphasizes the importance of identifying and stopping medications that cause syncope while also considering the necessity of treatments. Dehydration can exacerbate orthostatic tolerance issues, worsened by heat stress. Rehydration is recommended via oral or intravenous methods to aid recovery, with additional sodium supplementation suggested for more rapid recovery.

Recommendations.Statement: Reducing or withdrawing medications that may cause hypotension can be beneficial in selected patients with syncope.
Recommendations.Explanation: Medication-related syncope appears higher in older patients. Several drug classes like diuretics and sedatives have been implicated. Close supervision is required during adjustments to prevent worsening of conditions such as preexisting supine hypertension or cardiac arrhythmias.

Recommendations.Statement: In selected patients with syncope due to dehydration, it is reasonable to encourage increased salt and fluid intake.
Recommendations.Explanation: Sodium supplementation improves plasma volume and enhances orthostatic tolerance. This option is not suitable for patients with cardiac dysfunction, uncontrolled hypertension, or chronic kidney disease.

Key Points on Conditions.Description: Refers to symptoms developing upon standing and relieved by lying down. Common symptoms include lightheadedness, palpitations, and fatigue. It may be associated with hemodynamic disturbances.
Pathophysiology involves inappropriate tachycardia upon standing. Common in patients with elevated heart rates >120 bpm upon standing.

Characterized by a syndrome of apparent loss of consciousness without cerebral perfusion impairment. Often related to psychological stress and typically occurs in young females with a history of psychological or sexual abuse.

content.title: Recommendations for the Treatment of Pseudosyncope

recommendations.Description: In patients with suspected pseudosyncope, a candid discussion with the patient about the diagnosis may be reasonable. Some reports suggest that patients benefit from being informed of the suspected diagnosis in a clear but sympathetic manner that also acknowledges the involuntary nature of the attacks.

Cognitive behavioral therapy may be beneficial in patients with pseudosyncope. Uncontrolled studies suggest that psychotherapy, particularly cognitive behavioral therapy, may be beneficial in conversion disorders.

uncommon_conditions.title: 9. Uncommon Conditions Associated with Syncope
uncommon_conditions.description: Syncope has been reported in many uncommon diseases. Specific conditions may predispose the patient to various types of syncope. A list of less common conditions associated with syncope is provided for differential diagnosis.

conditions.name: Cardiac tamponade
conditions.notes: Often tachycardia and hypotension; may be hypotensive and bradycardic acutely.

conditions.name: Constrictive pericarditis
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Severe HF symptoms, including edema, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea.
conditions.notes: May be associated with cough syncope.

conditions.name: LV noncompaction
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Cardiomyopathy characterized by prominent LV trabeculae and deep intertrabecular recesses, due to embryologic perturbation.
conditions.notes: Syncope reported in 5%-9% of both adult and pediatric patients. The mechanism may be a tachyarrhythmia.

conditions.name: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Apical ballooning and basal hypercontractility, often due to stress. Chest pain and ECG changes consistent with ischemia are commonly seen.
conditions.notes: Syncope is uncommon and may be multifactorial.

conditions.name: Pulmonary embolus
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Hypoxemia, tachycardia; hypotension and shock leading to pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest in severe cases.
conditions.notes: Syncope due to bradycardia and/or hypotension. One study showed higher prevalence of pulmonary embolus in older patients with first episode of syncope after admission to the hospital.

conditions.name: Pulmonary arterial hypertension
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Occurs more often during exertion in younger patients.
conditions.notes: Syncope due to inability to augment or sustain cardiac output during exertion, followed by vasodilatation.

conditions.name: Fabry disease
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Lysosomal storage disorder with neuropathic pain, renal failure concurrent LVH, and HF.
conditions.notes: Syncope usually due to AV block.

conditions.name: Amyloidosis
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Systemic disease due to amyloid deposition. Light chain amyloidosis affects the kidneys, heart, and peripheral and autonomic nervous systems.
conditions.notes: Syncope may be due to conduction system disease, arrhythmias, impaired cardiac output from restrictive cardiomyopathy, or neurological involvement.

conditions.name: Hemochromatosis
conditions.clinical_characteristics: Systemic iron deposition causing liver disease, skin pigmentation, diabetes mellitus, arthropathy, impotence, and dilated cardiomyopathy.
conditions.notes: Myocardial involvement more common than sick sinus syndrome and AV conduction disease.

Conditions.Name: Myocarditis
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Chest pain, arrhythmias, or profound LV systolic dysfunction. Hemodynamic collapse may occur.
Conditions.Notes: VT and AV block are the likely causes of syncope; transient hemodynamic collapse is possible.

Conditions.Name: Lyme disease
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Lyme myocarditis with classical features of Lyme disease, including erythema migraines and neurological manifestations.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope may be due to AV block, but many patients manifest VVS.

Conditions.Name: Chagas disease
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Chagasic cardiomyopathy caused by trypanosomiasis.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope and sudden death associated with ventricular tachyarrhythmias. AV block also occurs.

Conditions.Name: Myotonic dystrophy
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Autosomal dominant inheritance with multiple organ systems affected. Grip myotonia, weakness, temporal wasting, alopecia, cataracts, glucose intolerance, and daytime somnolence.
Conditions.Notes: Both bradyarrhythmia and tachyarrhythmias.

Conditions.Name: Friedreich ataxia
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Autosomal recessive inheritance with limb and gait ataxia, bladder dysfunction, and daytime somnolence. Diffuse interstitial fibrosis and HCM.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope can be bradycardic or tachycardic. SCD is known to occur.

Conditions.Name: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Mitochondrial myopathy. Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, pigmentary retinopathy.
Conditions.Notes: Many patients develop significant His-Purkinje disease.

Conditions.Name: Erb dystrophy
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Limb girdle muscular dystrophy, manifesting as scapulohumeral and/or pelvicfemoral weakness and atrophy.
Conditions.Notes: AV conduction disease, dilated cardiomyopathy.

Conditions.Name: Lenegre-Lev disease
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Progressive fibrosis and sclerosis of cardiac conduction system, including the cardiac skeleton, including the aortic and mitral rings.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope is usually due to high-grade AV block.

Conditions.Name: Cardiac tumors
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Triad of obstruction, embolic, and systemic signs and symptoms.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope is often due to obstruction to blood flow.

Conditions.Name: Prosthetic valve thrombosis
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Ranges from asymptomatic to profound HF.
Conditions.Notes: May have similar presentation to a cardiac tumor, with a high risk of embolic phenomenon and obstruction.

Conditions.Name: Anomalous coronary artery
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Common cause of exertional syncope or SCD, classically in young athletes.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope can be due to Bezold Jarisch reflex, hypotension, VT, or AV block.

Conditions.Name: Aortic dissection
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Aortic dissection may manifest with neurological symptoms, myocardial infarction, and HF.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope can occur in as many as 13% of aortic dissections.

Conditions.Name: Subclavian steal
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: The phenomenon of flow reversal in a vertebral artery ipsilateral to a hemodynamically significant stenosis of the subclavian artery.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope is generally associated with upper-extremity activity.

Conditions.Name: Coarctation of the aorta
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: If severe, it can result in HF or aortic dissection.
Conditions.Notes: Associated bicuspid aortic valve stenosis may be considered with syncope.

Conditions.Name: Rheumatoid arthritis
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Chronic, autoimmune inflammatory disorder with systemic manifestations.
Conditions.Notes: Rarely associated with complete heart block and syncope.

Conditions.Name: Syringomyelia/Chiari malformation
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Arnold Chiari malformations are the most common form of syringomyelia.
Conditions.Notes: Syringomyelia-induced disruption of sympathetic fibers in the thoracic spinal cord is a rare mechanism of syncope.

Conditions.Name: Neck/vestigal tumor
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Recurrent syncope is an uncommon complication of neck malignancy.
Conditions.Notes: The mechanism may be invasion of the carotid sinus or the afferent nerve fibers of the glossopharyngeal nerve.

Conditions.Name: Carcinoid syndrome/Pheochromocytoma
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: These tumors can release vasoactive peptides and cause vasodilation, flushing, pruritus, and gastrointestinal symptoms.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope is usually due to transient hypotension.

Conditions.Name: Beta thalassemia major
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Severe anemia, multiple organ failure, and dilated cardiomyopathy due to iron overload.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope may be arrhythmic.

Conditions.Name: Seizure-induced bradycardia/hypotension
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Generally due to temporal lobe epilepsy.
Conditions.Notes: Postictal bradyarrhythmia is uncommon and likely originates from the temporal lobe or limbic system.

Conditions.Name: Migraine
Conditions.Clinical Characteristics: Migraine headaches are statistically associated with syncope.
Conditions.Notes: Syncope may be vasovagal or due to orthostatic intolerance.

genes.context: Autosomal dominant inheritance with multiple organ systems affected. Grip myotonia, weakness, temporal wasting, alopecia, cataracts, glucose intolerance, and daytime somnolence.
Autosomal recessive inheritance with limb and gait ataxia, bladder dysfunction, and daytime somnolence. Diffuse interstitial fibrosis and HCM.
Mitochondrial myopathy. Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, pigmentary retinopathy.
Limb girdle muscular dystrophy, manifesting as scapulohumeral and/or pelvicfemoral weakness and atrophy.

recommendations: VVS evaluation, including a detailed medical history, physical examination, family history, and a 12-lead ECG, should be performed in all pediatric patients presenting with syncope.
Noninvasive diagnostic testing should be performed in pediatric patients presenting with syncope and suspected CHD, cardiomyopathy, or primary rhythm disorder.

syncope in children.prevalence: 30% to 50% of children experience at least 1 fainting episode by 18 years of age.
syncope in children.incidence: Higher in females and peaks between 15 to 19 years of age.
types: Neurally mediated syncope accounts for 75% of pediatric syncope.
Breath-holding spells typically occur from age 6 months to age 5 years.
Pallid breath-holding spells are seen in the first 1 to 2 years of age.
syncope in children.causes: May result from obstruction to blood flow, myocardial dysfunction, or congenital coronary anomaly.

content.family history: A detailed family history should focus on premature SCD among first- and second-degree relatives.
content.risk factors: Include absence of prodromal symptoms, palpitations with seconds of loss of consciousness, history of SCD, abnormal physical examination, and abnormal ECG.
content.diagnosis: Channelopathies are major causes of cardiac-related syncope in young people. Extended monitoring is reasonable when an arrhythmia diagnosis is suspected.

genes.context: External syncope has been associated with LQT3 and CPVT.
External syncope has been associated with LQT3 and CPVT.
Interventions recommended for adults with similar conditions presenting with syncope can be applied in children.

content.Management of Children with VVS: Management should include reassurance about the benign nature of VVS, emphasizing symptom awareness and avoiding triggers like dehydration and standing for prolonged periods.
content.Tilt-Table Testing: Useful for pediatric patients when diagnosis is unclear; sensitivity and specificity ranges are provided. Related to seizures and asystolic pauses.
content.Midodrine Use: Recommended if VVS is not responding to lifestyle measures.
content.Salt and Fluid Intake: Encouraged in selected pediatric patients with VVS.
content.Fludrocortisone: Effectiveness is uncertain in pediatric patients with OH associated with syncope. Mixed study results.
content.Cardiac Pacing: May be considered in severe neurally mediated syncope related to breath-holding spells.
content.Beta Blockers: Not beneficial in pediatric patients with VVS.

ACHD recommendations.Recommendation: For evaluation of patients with ACHD and syncope, referral to a specialist with expertise in ACHD can be beneficial.
EPS is reasonable in patients with moderate or severe ACHD and unexplained syncope.

content.Context: The guidelines provide recommendations for the management of Adult Congenital Heart Disease (ACHD), focusing on the need for specialized care and electrophysiological studies (EPS) in patients with syncope. Special considerations are given to mortality rates, arrhythmias, and specific patient groups such as older patients.

Recommendations for Geriatric Patients.Recommendation: For the assessment and management of older adults with syncope, a comprehensive approach in collaboration with an expert in geriatric care can be beneficial.
It is reasonable to consider syncope as a cause of nonaccidental falls in older adults.

Key Points: A multidisciplinary approach is beneficial in diagnosing frailty and other factors in older adults with syncope.
Consideration of frailty includes weight loss, weakness, exhaustion, reduced physical activity, and cognitive decline.
Approximately 30% of older adults with nonaccidental falls have had syncope.
Driving considerations should balance the risk of syncope with the need for driving.
The estimated acceptable risk of syncope while driving is less than 0.0017%.

recommendations.recommendation: It can be beneficial for healthcare providers managing patients with syncope to know the driving laws and restrictions in their regions and discuss implications with the patient.

additional_information: The writing committee encourages healthcare providers to be aware of regional driving laws and restrictions.
In the United States, private driving is state regulated, while commercial driving requires a federal license.
Physicians providing care to commercial drivers should be familiar with U.S. Department of Transportation policy.
Individual states may require reporting of drivers who faint, and this should be considered regardless of restriction duration.
Patient confidentiality should be maintained unless the condition poses a significant risk to others.

symptom_free_waiting_times.VVS, >6 syncope per year: Not fit to drive until symptoms resolved

content.summary: The guideline discusses syncope in athletes, focusing on its predominantly vasovagal origin. It highlights the increased probability of cardiac causes during exercise, the importance of thorough evaluation, and the need for cardiovascular assessment. Recommendations include screening, evaluations, and monitoring, especially for those with high-risk cardiovascular indications.

recommendations.recommendation: Cardiovascular assessment by an experienced care provider is recommended for athletes with syncope before resuming sports.
Assessment by a specialist is reasonable for athletes with syncope and high-risk markers.
Extended monitoring is beneficial for athletes with unexplained syncope after initial evaluation.
Participation in sports is not recommended for athletes with syncope and phenotype-positive HCM, CPVT, LQTS1, or ARVC without specialist evaluation.

Quality of Life and Healthcare Cost of S.Impact of Syncope on Quality of Life: Quality of life (QoL) is reduced with recurrent syncope, which affects physical health and mental health, including increased fear and anxiety. It also impacts daily activities like driving and working.
Quality of Life and Healthcare Cost of S.Healthcare Costs Associated with Syncope: High costs are incurred in the evaluation and management of syncope, with annual hospital costs estimated to be $4.1 billion in 2014 dollars in the U.S.

Emerging Technology, Evidence Gaps, and.Definition, Classification, and Epidemio: Variability in prevalence and incidence due to different methodologies and settings is noted. Standard definitions are needed for future studies.
Emerging Technology, Evidence Gaps, and.Risk Stratification and Clinical Outcome: Key clinical questions include determining underlying causes of syncope and related outcomes, and identifying effective therapies to prevent recurrence and related impacts.

Studies Needed: Determine if syncope is an independent predictor of nonfatal or fatal outcomes.
Develop risk scores for clinical settings with predefined endpoints.
Define relevant clinical outcomes for recurrent syncope, nonfatal outcomes, and fatal outcomes.
Differentiate cardiac and noncardiac clinical outcomes in various settings.
Understand the interaction and relationships among syncope symptoms, cause, and underlying conditions.
Evaluation and Diagnosis: Improve effectiveness of patient evaluation based on risk.
Understand key components of clinical characteristics and develop standardization tools.
Management of Specific Conditions: Treatment straightforward for cardiac causes of syncope or orthostatic causes.
Differentiate between 'arrhythmic syncope' and 'nonarrhythmic syncope' versus 'aborted SCD'.
Identify effective treatment approaches for patients with recurrent reflex syncope.
Special Populations: Questions and research on risk stratification, evaluation, and management in pediatric and geriatric populations and athletes.
Risk associated with driving among different populations with syncope.
Usefulness of specialized syncope units in different clinical settings.

content.general_clinical_information: The document outlines various studies and methods related to the evaluation and management of syncope in emergency departments. It references several tools and scores, such as the San Francisco Syncope Rule, the ROSE (Risk Stratification of Syncope in the Emergency Department) study, and the CHADS2 risk score, among others. These tools aim to predict outcomes and stratify risk for patients presenting with syncope, particularly focusing on short-term and long-term clinical outcomes, mortality, and specific underlying causes.

content.guideline: This document is the 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline. It covers clinical guidelines related to the evaluation and management of syncope, which includes sudden cardiac death, cardiac imaging, electrocardiography, and ambulatory monitoring. The guideline discusses the use of various diagnostic tools such as MRI, CT scans, Holter monitoring, and implantable loop recorders among others for patients experiencing syncope or potential cardiac conditions. It also evaluates different cardiac markers.

content.content: The guideline discusses various aspects of syncope, emphasizing the role of implantable loop recorders, diagnostic strategies, and clinical evaluations. It highlights the utility of different testing methods including electrophysiologic studies and tilt-table tests. The focus is on understanding underlying causes and improving diagnostic yield for unexplained syncope, especially in elderly populations. There is an exploration of associated medical conditions, management recommendations, and advancements in diagnostic technologies.

General Clinical Information: The document discusses various aspects of cardiac conditions, specifically focusing on ventricular arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, and related syndromes such as Brugada syndrome. It covers topics like the efficacy of implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs), corticosteroid therapy, and ablation techniques in managing these conditions. There is also mention of epidemiological data and risk stratification related to cardiac sarcoidosis and other heart-related conditions.

content.topic: The guideline focuses on syncope, particularly in relation to implantable cardioverter-defibrillators, genetic predispositions, and certain cardiac conditions.
key_areas: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) and its management.
Genetic factors influencing cardiac conditions and syncope.
Medications and therapeutic strategies for managing cardiac conditions linked to syncope.

genes.context: Mutations in the cardiac ryanodine receptor gene (RYR2) underlie catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. Studies have shown that these mutations can lead to arrhythmic risk during physical activity.

A missense mutation in a highly conserved region of CASQ2 is associated with autosomal recessive catecholamine-induced polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. This genetic change influences calcium handling in cardiac cells.

content.Title: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline
content.Overview: The document contains various studies and guidelines regarding syncope, including efficacy and safety of different drugs and interventions, as well as preventive and therapeutic strategies for vasovagal and neurocardiogenic syncope.
Key Topics: Prevention and management of vasovagal syncope
Efficacy of midodrine, metoprolol, and other treatments
Impact of lifestyle changes such as fluid and salt intake

General Information: The guideline discusses various treatments and studies related to syncope, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, and orthostatic hypotension.
Several studies focus on pacing in patients with carotid sinus syndrome and the effects of different interventions on orthostatic hypotension.
Comparative studies are mentioned for pacing methods, drug effects, and physiological responses to orthostatic challenges.
The guideline references multiple randomized trials, double-blind studies, and cohort studies to discuss effective treatments and management strategies.

general_info: The document addresses various treatments and studies related to orthostatic hypotension and syncope.
Several drugs and treatment methods are discussed, such as droxidopa for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension and the use of somatostatin analogues.
The importance of managing syncope in different conditions like autonomic failure, Parkinson's disease, and diabetes is emphasized.
Clinical trials and studies are referenced to support different treatment approaches.

genes.context: Mutations in SCN5A gene can lead to Brugada syndrome and Long QT syndrome, which are associated with syncope and sudden cardiac death.

context: HCM indicates hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; it's listed under cardiac or vascular nonarrhythmic conditions that may require further evaluation.
Exertion can result in syncope in a variety of conditions...such as LQTS (type 1) and catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT).
LQTS is diagnosed in the presence of a pathogenic mutation in 1 of the LQTS genes.
Mutation in the gene encoding the cardiac ryanodine receptor (RyR2) (autosomal dominant) or in the cardiac calsequestrin gene (CASQ2) (autosomal recessive) is linked to CPVT.

Mutations in the cardiac ryanodine receptor gene (RYR2) underlie catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. Studies have shown that these mutations can lead to arrhythmic risk during physical activity.
Mutation in the gene encoding the cardiac ryanodine receptor (RyR2) (autosomal dominant) or in the cardiac calsequestrin gene (CASQ2) (autosomal recessive) is linked to CPVT.

A missense mutation in a highly conserved region of CASQ2 is associated with autosomal recessive catecholamine-induced polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. This genetic change influences calcium handling in cardiac cells.
Autosomal dominant inheritance with multiple organ systems affected. Grip myotonia, weakness, temporal wasting, alopecia, cataracts, glucose intolerance, and daytime somnolence.
Autosomal recessive inheritance with limb and gait ataxia, bladder dysfunction, and daytime somnolence. Diffuse interstitial fibrosis and HCM.
Mitochondrial myopathy. Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, pigmentary retinopathy.
Limb girdle muscular dystrophy, manifesting as scapulohumeral and/or pelvicfemoral weakness and atrophy.
External syncope has been associated with LQT3 and CPVT.
External syncope has been associated with LQT3 and CPVT.
Interventions recommended for adults with similar conditions presenting with syncope can be applied in children.
Mutations in SCN5A gene can lead to Brugada syndrome and Long QT syndrome, which are associated with syncope and sudden cardiac death.
